summary:
Labyrinthectomy has been shown effective for treating refractory vertigo due to Meniere’s disease through multiple studies indicating reduced frequency of disabling attacks; however, this comes at the cost of potential irreversible loss of hearing function post-surgery. Given persistent symptoms despite conservative treatments, labyrinthectomy remains an appropriate option when aiming primarily to alleviate severe vertigo impacting quality-of-life significantly more than any residual auditory impairment might affect such individuals. To summarize concisely based solely upon your request,

1) **Labyrinthectomy** demonstrates efficacy against resistant vertigo from Menière’s but involves risk of permanent single-sided deafness,
2) It should be considered if conventional therapies fail as primary relief strategy focusing heavily on reducing symptom burden rather than preserving full audiological capacity. This aligns closely with prioritizing functional improvement via elimination or reduction of incapacitating episodic dizziness events characteristic of advanced cases unresponsive to prior interventions. Agent2 concluded their response emphasizing both benefits—significant alleviation of vertigo—and drawbacks—invasive nature leading to one side losing all sense of sound perception—which together suggest careful deliberation before proceeding towards definitive treatment options tailored per individual needs balancing between symptomatic control versus maintaining pre-existing sensory
